answer:
**Śrāddha** (श्राद्ध, also spelled *sraddhā*), known as the rite for ancestors (*pitṛkṛya* or *pitṛkarman*), is a central funerary and commemorative ritual in the Smṛti tradition. It involves offerings (*piṇḍa*, food balls; water libations/*tarpaṇa*; cooked foods) to deceased paternal ancestors (*pitṛs*: father, grandfather, great-grandfather, and extended lineage) to ensure their satisfaction (*tṛpti*), nourishment in the afterlife, and blessings of progeny, wealth, and longevity for the performer.[^1][^2] Performed primarily by householders (*gṛhasthas*), it is one of the *pañcamahāyajñas* (five great sacrifices), alongside rites to gods (*deva*), sages (*ṛṣi*), beings (*bhūta*), and men (*manuṣya*).[^3]

### Core Elements and Procedure (from Manu and Āṅgirasa)
The *Manusmṛti* (Ch. 3–4) provides the archetypal description, emphasizing purity, timing, and structure:
- **Preparation**: Performed in a pure, south-sloping spot plastered with cow dung. Brahmin guests (*snātakas*, unblemished) are seated on kuśa grass seats, honored with perfumes/garlands. Fire oblation (*homa*) to Agni-Soma-Yama precedes ancestral offerings.[^4]
- **Offerings**: Three *piṇḍas* (rice/sesame balls) offered south-facing with mantras (*svadhā*). Foods: hot, silent meals of milk-rice, roots/fruits, meats (e.g., fish=2 months satisfaction; rhinoceros=12 years; *kālaśāka*=eternal). Avoid tears/anger (pollutes offerings).[^5]
- **Dismissal and boons**: Brahmins bless (*svadhā*), performer prays for progeny/wealth. Leftovers (*ucchṣṭa*) for dogs/crows; *piṇḍas* fed to cow/fire/water.[^6]
- **Who performs**: Sons (even if father alive, for prior ancestors); daughter's son preferred. Wife eats middle *piṇḍa* for virtuous son.[^7]

The *Āṅgirasa Smṛti* (Ch. 1, its dominant theme) expands on impediments (*pratibandha*): ritual impurity (*sūtaka*) post-cooking doesn't halt; prioritize over vows/penances/sacrifices (do sacrifice first if same day); perform despite obstacles (e.g., death nearby post-cooking). Monthly/death-anniversary (*mṛtāha*)/new moon (*pārvaṇa*) obligatory; options for living father (e.g., *piṇḍapitṛyajña*).[^8][^9]

### Timing and Occasions
- **Daily/monthly**: New/full moon (*darśa-pūrṇamāsa*), *aṣṭakā*, *anvaṣṭakā*.
- **Annual**: Death anniversaries, *pitṛpakṣa* (dark fortnight).
- **Auspicious**: Afternoon/dark fortnight (10th–13th tithis); avoid nights/twilights/eclipses/menstruation.[^10]
- *Nāndī/sodakumbha*: Pre-rites variants.

### Foods and Duration of Satisfaction
| Food                  | Ancestors Satisfied |
|-----------------------|---------------------|
| Sesame/rice/barley   | 1 month            |
| Fish                 | 2 months           |
| Deer/mutton/birds    | 3–5 months         |
| Goat/spotted deer    | 6–7 months         |
| Antelope/ruru        | 8–9 months         |
| Boar/buffalo         | 10–11 months       |
| Milk-rice/rhinoceros | 1 year/12 years    |
| *Kālaśāka*/hermit foods/honey-13th tithi | Eternal[^11] |

### Variations and Prohibitions Across Texts
- **Viṣṇu Smṛti** (Ch. 64+): *Tarpaṇa* with *pitṛya tīrtha* (forefinger base); echoes Manu on purity.[^12]
- **Yājñavalkya Smṛti** (Ch. 1): Brief; stresses honey/ghee for *pitṛs*.[^13]
- **Prohibitions**: No Śūdra involvement; reverse parent order redo rite; oil-bath invalidates some (*sodakumbha*).[^14]
- **Related rites**: *Piṇḍapitṛyajña* (if father alive); *nāndī śrāddha* (pre-wedding).

Not every Smṛti (e.g., Bṛhaspati, Kātyāyana focus law) details it equally; *Āṅgirasa* and *Manusmṛti* are richest. The rite upholds *pitṛṛṇa* (ancestor debt), ensuring lineage continuity.[^15]

[^1]: Āṅgirasa Smṛti, Ch. 1, [p-33e942](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-33e942).
[^2]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 3, [p-d8ebfe](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/03/translation/#p-d8ebfe) (contextual; full rite from ~Mn_3.204).
[^3]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 4, [p-7a3359](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/04/translation/#p-7a3359).
[^4]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 4 wiki key passage [p-8b8ea4](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/04/translation/#p-8b8ea4).
[^5]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 4, [p-c7d753](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/04/translation/#p-c7d753) (foods).
[^6]: Ibid., [p-42536e](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/03/translation/#p-42536e).
[^7]: Āṅgirasa Smṛti, Ch. 1, [p-78e3a5](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-78e3a5).
[^8]: Ibid., [p-bf0738](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-bf0738).
[^9]: Ibid., [p-d9e021](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-d9e021).
[^10]: Manusmṛti, Ch. 4, [p-7a3359](/works/unknown-manusmrti-347b76/04/translation/#p-7a3359).
[^11]: Ibid.
[^12]: Viṣṇu Smṛti, Ch. 64, [p-1983a9](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/64/translation/#p-1983a9).
[^13]: Yājñavalkya Smṛti, Ch. 1 wiki [p-47ada8](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/01/translation/#p-47ada8).
[^14]: Āṅgirasa Smṛti, Ch. 1, [p-285ddb](/works/unknown-angirasa-smrti-2bf9d1/01/translation/#p-285ddb).
[^15]: Full Ch. 1 Āṅgirasa wiki: "*śrāddha* श्राद्ध ang_1" top trigram.
